hook: Cantonese canteen long famed as the rudest dining room in London
facts: Occupies 41-43 Wardour Street over four storeys, seating around 500
Refurbished under new ownership in 2014; trades on a sixth-decade legacy
Cash-and-cheap Cantonese roast meats, congee and dumplings
legend: Legend says waiters once barked 'upstairs!', snatched plates and chased anyone tipping short, and that asking for a knife and fork earned a public scolding
w: Four storeys, 500 seats, and a reputation as the rudest dining room in town. They say waiters once barked 'upstairs!', snatched your plate and chased short tippers down Wardour Street; ask for a fork and get scolded. Tamer since 2014, the swagger lingers. Order the roast duck and behave.

hook: Gerrard Street trolley dim sum, the old-school Chinatown ritual
facts: 28-29 Gerrard Street, a long-running Cantonese banquet hall
Dim sum served daily until late afternoon, last orders around 4:45pm
Big rowdy room favoured by Chinatown's Cantonese diaspora
w: Carts rattle between the tables, lids lift on steaming bamboo, and the whole rowdy ritual of trolley dim sum survives here when half of London has gone a la carte. Char siu bao, har gau, cheung fun, ticked off a paper slip. Cantonese to the core, gloriously loud. Get there before the carts stop at five.

hook: Roast duck the FT once called the best in the world
facts: 12 Gerrard Street, the Chinatown branch of the duck specialists
Ducks reared free-roaming at Silver Hill Farm in Ireland
Plain noisy room, famously brusque service, queues for the bird
legend: Reputation that the kitchen plays soft music to the ducks to relax the meat
w: Lacquered ducks hang in the window, skin like burnished glass, the meat a Financial Times pick for best on earth. They say the birds get soft music to keep them calm before the oven. Plain room, charmless service, perpetual queue, nobody cares. Crisp skin, tender flesh, secret spice. Order the duck.

hook: Sichuan street snacks and steamed baozi, retro propaganda decor
facts: Opened 2008 on Newport Court; now trades at 24 Romilly Street
Known for dan dan noodles, baozi and Sichuan small plates
Walls hung with Mao-era posters and old enamel signs
w: Pillowy steamed baozi and tongue-numbing dan dan noodles in a snug room papered with Mao-era posters and old enamel adverts. Started on Newport Court in 2008, an early voice for proper Sichuan street snacking before the West End caught on, now tucked on Romilly Street. Order the pork buns and the dan dan.

hook: The original ginger-and-scallion lobster noodle, since 1978
facts: 14-16 Queensway, opposite Queensway station, opened 1978
Claims to have invented London's lobster noodle dish
Seafood-heavy Cantonese menu, dated carpeted room
w: Behind the dated carpet and brusque waiters, a kitchen that claims to have invented London's lobster noodle back in 1978: sweet shellfish over ginger-and-scallion egg noodles that soak up every drop of sauce. Soft-shell crab and salted-egg prawns keep the regulars loyal. Bayswater's seafood institution. Bring a wallet.

hook: Xinjiang dumplings and big-plate chicken at shared wooden benches
facts: 49 Camberwell Church Street, family-run Xinjiang specialist
Cult following for da pan ji and cumin-heavy lamb
Long communal tables; reopened in 2024 after a closure
w: Xinjiang cooking from the far western edge of China, served at long shared benches in a no-frills Camberwell room: hand-pinched dumplings, cumin-soaked lamb, and da pan ji, the big-plate chicken hauled out for the whole table. A south London cult, shuttered then triumphantly reopened in 2024. Go hungry, share everything.

hook: Xi'an hand-pulled biang biang noodles down a Bloomsbury alley
facts: 13 Cosmo Place, a tiny pedestrian lane off Southampton Row
First solo restaurant of Xi'an Impression chef Guirong Wei
Famous for biang biang noodles, liangpi and the pork burger
w: Down a flagstoned Bloomsbury alley, chef Guirong Wei slaps biang biang noodles wide as belts and dishes up the cumin-lamb pork burger that built her name at Xi'an Impression. Cold liangpi, fiery oil, the food of the old Silk Road terminus. Cramped, queued, worth it. Order the hand-pulled noodles.

hook: Xi'an murgers and dumplings, the flagship by Euston
facts: Flagship at 62 Eversholt Street, opened 2014
Named for the 'murger', Xi'an's slow-cooked meat flatbread
Biang biang noodles, wontons and dumplings round the menu
w: The name nods to the murger, Xi'an's answer to a burger: spiced slow-cooked meat crammed into a crisp griddled flatbread. Opened by Euston in 2014, it spread the gospel of biang biang noodles and proper northern dumplings across town. Glass-noodle soups, wontons, chilli oil everywhere. Come for the flatbread, stay for the noodles.

hook: Georgian khinkali soup dumplings, twisted by hand and eaten by the knot
facts: 87 Goldsmiths Row, a long-running family Georgian restaurant
Khinkali: broth-filled dumplings pinched into a topknot
Bring-your-own-bottle with a small corkage fee
legend: Tradition holds you grip the doughy knot, never eat it, and count the empties to tally who ate most
w: Georgian cooking off a Hackney back street: khinkali, fat soup dumplings pinched into a doughy topknot you grip but never eat. Tradition says you pile the knots to tally who ate most. Cheese-boat khachapuri, walnut everything, bring your own wine. Pierce, slurp the broth, repeat.

hook: Market-stall sheng jian bao, pan-fried and bursting with broth
facts: Permanent counter at Old Spitalfields Market after years on stalls
Signature sheng jian bao: pan-fried soup-filled pork buns
Founders travel to China to refine the recipes
w: From a roving market stall to a permanent counter under the Spitalfields canopy, built on the sheng jian bao: pork buns pan-fried crisp on the bottom, soft on top, a scalding slurp of broth sealed inside. Sichuan chicken and chilli wontons alongside. Bite the corner, sip the soup, do not wear white.

hook: Handmade Tibetan momos off the old Truman Brewery yard
facts: 7 Dray Walk, between Brick Lane and the Truman Brewery
Family-run, serving handmade momos since 2008
Steamed and fried momos with fiery momo sauce
w: Tucked on Dray Walk by the old Truman Brewery, a family kitchen pleating Tibetan momos by hand since 2008: half-moon dumplings of pork or beef or veg, steamed or fried, slicked in a chilli-tomato momo sauce that earns its place. Thukpa noodle soup to follow. Plateau cooking on the Brick Lane fringe.

hook: Nepalese steamed momos under a Borough railway arch
facts: Arch 229, Bank End, in the Borough Yards arches by the river
Opened 2024 by sisters Trishna and Dipa Chamling
Specialist Nepali-style steamed and fried dumplings
w: Under a Borough railway arch, sisters Trishna and Dipa Chamling pleat Nepalese momos to order: juicy half-moons of chicken, pork or veg, steamed or crisped, served with the nutty-spicy achar that Kathmandu swears by. A bright little canteen squeezed between the Borough Yards stones. Steamed, dunked, gone in seconds.

hook: London's first bubble-tea lab, shakers and lab coats since 2011
facts: Original branch at 49 Rupert Street, opened 2011
Among the first dedicated boba shops in the city
Teas shaken over ice; staff in lab coats, flasks in the window
w: London's bubble-tea pioneer dressed as a chemistry set: flasks bubbling in the window, chalkboard formulae on the walls, staff in lab coats shaking milk tea over ice like cocktails. Opened on Rupert Street in 2011, before boba lined every high street. Tapioca pearls optional, theatre included. Order it shaken.

hook: The homegrown UK boba chain born in Camden's Stables Market
facts: First shop opened in Camden in 2012; now over 100 UK branches
Camden site sits in the Stables Market off Chalk Farm Road
Fresh-brewed milk and fruit teas with tapioca pearls
w: Britain's own boba story started here in Camden in 2012, a single counter that bred a hundred-shop chain. The original still pours among the cobbles and railway arches of the Stables Market, milk tea shaken fresh, tapioca sunk to the bottom of the cup. Homegrown bubble tea, brewed where it began. Get the brown sugar.

hook: Taiwanese fruit tea, the brand's first UK shop on Chinatown's edge
facts: 100 Shaftesbury Avenue, the chain's debut UK branch
Built on hand-squeezed sugarcane and pineapple fruit teas
Part of the boba boom along the Chinatown lanes
w: Taiwan's fruit-tea specialist chose the Chinatown edge of Shaftesbury Avenue for its first British shop, trading on hand-squeezed sugarcane and a winter-melon brew steeped the slow way. Less neon than its boba neighbours, more about the tea than the pearls. Order the pineapple green and watch them pour.
